topic: Müller, K., & Zhang, L. (2023). Quantifying confabulation in autoregressive language models: The confabulation coefficient and cascade effects. Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research, 78(4), 1123–1145. doi:10.48550/arXiv.2305.12345
final_review:
The concept of confabulation in autoregressive language models has gained significant attention in recent years, with researchers attempting to quantify and understand its implications. One approach to detecting confabulation is through the use of semantic entropy, as proposed by Farquhar et al. (2024). This method has been shown to be effective in identifying hallucinations in large language models (Farquhar et al., 2024). Similarly, Rawte et al. (2023) have provided an extensive definition and quantification of hallucination in large language models, highlighting the need for prescriptive remediations (Rawte et al., 2023).

In contrast, some researchers have argued that confabulation can have surprising value in certain contexts, such as in the generation of creative text (Sui et al., 2024). This perspective is supported by the work of Sui et al. (2024), who demonstrate the potential benefits of large language model hallucinations. However, others have raised concerns about the potential risks of confabulation, particularly in high-stakes applications such as medical imaging (Bradshaw et al., 2025).

The use of large language models in healthcare has also been explored, with Tam et al. (2024) proposing a framework for human evaluation of these models (Tam et al., 2024). This work highlights the need for careful consideration of the potential risks and benefits of large language models in healthcare applications. In addition, research has shown that the role of belief in occurrence within autobiographical memory can have significant implications for our understanding of confabulation (Scoboria et al., 2013).

The study of confabulation has also been informed by research in neuroscience, with Vocat et al. (2010) investigating the clinical-anatomical correlates of anosognosia for hemiplegia (Vocat et al., 2010). Furthermore, the work of Werner (2010) has explored the conceptual implications of fractals in the nervous system for theoretical neuroscience (Werner, 2010). In a broader context, the predictive brain hypothesis has been proposed as a framework for understanding cognitive science, with implications for our understanding of confabulation (Clark, 2013).

In conclusion, the study of confabulation in autoregressive language models is a complex and multifaceted field, with a range of perspectives and approaches. While some researchers have highlighted the potential risks of confabulation, others have argued for its surprising value in certain contexts. Further research is needed to fully understand the implications of confabulation and to develop effective strategies for mitigating its risks.
